1: en tō elthein auton eis oikon tinos tōn archontōn tōn Pharisaiōn sabbatō phagein arton — the construction en tō + infinitive is Luke's favored temporal frame (also 3:21; 9:18; 9:29); here it introduces a Sabbath dinner at the house of a ruling Pharisee. Archontōn (rulers, leaders — from archō = rule) denotes a senior Pharisee; this is not an ordinary householder but a prominent figure in the local community. autoi ēsan paratērumenoi auton — they were watching him — paratēreō (watch carefully, observe closely — from para- + tēreō = keep, guard; watching alongside) has a hostile surveillance nuance throughout Luke (6:7; 20:20); they are monitoring for violations, not simply curious.
2: anthrōpos tis ēn hydrōpikos — a certain man was dropsical — hydrōpikos (suffering from dropsy, abnormal fluid retention — from hydōr = water) is a hapax legomenon in the NT; this is the only occurrence of the term in the entire NT. Luke's medical vocabulary (cf. puretos at 4:38-39; lepra at 5:12; paralytikos at 5:24) reflects the tradition of his medical interest. Dropsy (hydrops in ancient medicine) is excess fluid in the body, often a symptom of cardiac, renal, or liver failure; visible, chronic, and socially limiting in an honor-shame society.
3: exestin tō sabbatō therapeusai ē ou — is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not — Jesus's direct question short-circuits the trap; instead of acting first and being accused, he poses the legal question to the legal experts. Exesti (it is lawful, permissible) is the standard Sabbath-dispute verb (also 6:2, 4, 9; 14:3); the question places the burden of answer on the Pharisees. The ē ou (or not) forces a binary judgment where any answer creates legal difficulty for them.
4: hoi de hēsychasan — they remained silent — hēsychasan (aorist of hēsychazō = be quiet, be still; also 23:56 for the women resting on the Sabbath) signals deliberate non-response. The legal experts who are there to trap Jesus cannot answer his question without entangling themselves. epilabomenos iāsato auton kai apelysen — taking hold, he healed him and released him — epilambanō (take hold of, grasp) is the deliberate physical contact that initiates healing; apolyō (release, let go, send away) dismisses the man after healing, ending the episode cleanly.
5: tinos hymōn huios ē bous eis phrear peseitai — whose son or ox falls into a well — the a fortiori argument (qal wahomer): if you rescue an animal on the Sabbath, how much more rescue a person? Textual variants exist: some manuscripts read onos (donkey) instead of huios (son); the donkey-ox pair matches Matt 12:11 more closely, while son-ox is the harder reading (more dramatic and therefore more likely original). Phrear (pit, well — also John 4:11-12 for Jacob's well) is the specific emergency that Sabbath law already acknowledged: danger to life supersedes Sabbath restrictions.
6: kai ouk ischusan antapokrithēnai pros tauta — they were not able to reply against these things — antapokrinomai (answer in return, reply against — compound: anti- + apo- + krinomai) describes a formal legal counter-argument; they cannot mount one. Pros tauta (against these things) — the demonstration of Sabbath mercy logic has left them without legal ground. The silence of v.4 (strategic) and the inability of v.6 (logical) together constitute Luke's portrait of religious expertise that cannot answer Jesus.
7: elegon de pros tous keklēmenous parabolēn — he told a parable to those who were invited — parabolē (parable — from paraballō = throw alongside, compare) is the comparison-teaching form. epechōn pōs tas prōtoklisias exelegonto — noticing how they chose the places of honor — epechō (hold toward, pay attention to) + prōtoklisia (first-reclining-place, place of honor at table — from prōtos + klisia = first leaning-position); the choice of table position is an honor-competition visible to all observers. The dinner table in Mediterranean antiquity is a theater of social rank.
8: mē kataklithēs eis tēn prōtoklisian — do not recline at the place of honor — kataklithēs (aorist passive subjunctive of kataklinō = make recline, recline) is the formal dining posture. Mēpote entimoteros sou ē keklēmenos hyp autou — lest a more honorable person than you has been invited — entimos (honored, esteemed — from en + timē = in honor) frames the social comparison: one's position at table must match one's social ranking, and presuming too high a position creates public shame when corrected.
9: dos toutō topon — give this person your place — topos (place) is both the physical seat and the social position; the displacement is public and shaming. meta aischunēs ton eschaton topon katechein — to take the last place with shame — aischunē (shame, disgrace — the social negative of timē/honor) is the emotion of public dishonoring; eschaton topon (last place) is the social nadir. The sequence is: presumption → public correction → shame, the honor-shame culture's social worst-case.
10: anabase epanō — move up higher — the ascending movement (anabainō = go up) in response to the host's invitation reverses the shame dynamic. tote estai soi doxa — then there will be glory/honor for you — doxa (glory, honor, repute — the highest honor-word) in the presence of all the other guests (enōpion pantōn tōn sunanakeimenenōn); the public scope of the honor is what makes it worth having. The parable maps the honor-culture's logic but redirects it: humble positioning before the host, not strategic self-promotion, wins honor.
11: hoti pas ho hypsōn heauton tapeinōthēsetai kai ho tapeinōn heauton hypsōthēsetai — for everyone who exalts himself will be humbled and everyone who humbles himself will be exalted — this is Luke's recurring reversal formula (also 18:14; cf. 1:52); it occurs in Matthew without the pas (all/everyone). Hypsoō (exalt, lift high) and tapeinoō (humble, lower) are antonymous divine-action verbs in the LXX (Ezek 21:26; Job 5:11; Ps 75:7); the divine passive (future passives = God acts) declares that the reversals are not social happenstance but divine governance.
12: mē phōnei tous philous sou mēde tous adelphous sou — do not invite your friends or your brothers — Jesus redefines the logic of hospitality from reciprocal exchange to non-reciprocal gift. The standard hospitality pattern in Mediterranean culture was charis-exchange: give to receive; the invited guest becomes socially obligated (antapodoma soi genētai = there will be a return-gift to you). Jesus's counter-instruction breaks the reciprocity cycle that structures the social order of honor exchange.
13: ptōchous anapeirous chōlous typhlous kale — invite the poor, crippled, lame, blind — the four categories (ptōchos, anapeiros, chōlos, typhlos) are the same grouping that appear in the Great Banquet parable (v.21) and mirror the eschatological inclusion list of Isa 35:5-6 and Isa 61:1-2. Anapeiros (crippled, disabled — from ana- + pēros = maimed) appears only here and v.21 in the NT; it denotes a disability that prevents participation in normal social and economic life.
14: makarios de esē hoti ouk echousin antapodounai soi — blessed are you because they cannot repay you — the beatitude (makarios) reframes the hospitality calculation: absence of repayment-ability is the condition of blessing, not a liability. antapodothēsetai gar soi en tē anastasei tōn dikaiōn — it will be repaid to you at the resurrection of the righteous — antapodidōmi (repay, recompense — compound of anti- + apo- + didōmi) is the reciprocity verb, but relocated to the eschaton: the divine repayment at the resurrection replaces the immediate social return. Anastasis tōn dikaiōn (resurrection of the righteous) reflects the Jewish apocalyptic expectation of a selective first resurrection (Dan 12:2; Acts 24:15).
15: makarios hostis phagetai arton en tē basileia tou theou — blessed is the one who will eat bread in the kingdom of God — the table-guest's exclamation combines the makarios-formula with the kingdom-feast motif (Isa 25:6; also 13:29); it is a pious cliché that Jesus will immediately reframe. Phagetai arton (eat bread — idiomatic for "share a meal") in the eschatological context echoes the messianic banquet tradition in Second Temple Jewish texts (1 Enoch 62:14; 4 Ezra 7:36). Jesus's parable response implies: the question is not whether the feast happens but whether the invited will show up.
16: anthrōpos tis epoiei deipnon mega kai ekalesen pollous — a certain man made a great banquet and invited many — deipnon (the main meal, dinner — the evening banquet; contrast ariston = morning meal) is the formal feast. The mega (great) qualifier signals extravagance and preparation. Pollous (many) identifies the invited group as a large, pre-arranged guest list; in Middle Eastern practice, formal invitations were extended in advance, with a second summons at the time of the meal to confirm attendance.
17: erchou hoti ēdē hetoima estin panta — come, because everything is now ready — the two-stage invitation structure (invitation in advance + the servant's summons at serving-time) is the standard of formal ancient hospitality; the second summons is the moment of actual commitment. Refusing the second summons after accepting the first is a serious social offense — it dishonors the host publicly. Ēdē hetoima (already ready) emphasizes that the delay is purely on the guests' side; the feast is prepared and waiting.
18: kai ērxanto apo mias pantes paraiteisthai — they all alike began to make excuses — apo mias (from one, all at once — possibly mias gnōmēs = one mind; from one voice/accord) is a Semitic idiom for unanimity. Paraiteisthai (beg off, excuse oneself — from para- + aiteomai = request alongside; to ask to be excused) is the polite refusal verb. The first excuse: agron ēgorasa (I bought a field) + exelthein idein (I need to go see it) — the field was already purchased; this is not urgent, merely convenient.
19: zeugē boōn ēgorasa pente kai poreuomai dokimasai auta — I bought five yoke of oxen and I'm going to try them out — zeugē boōn (yoke of oxen — zeugos = paired/yoked; a yoke of oxen is a working pair) × 5 = ten oxen, a large agricultural investment. Dokimasai (test, examine, prove — from dokimazō; the word for evaluating a coin's genuineness) implies field-testing new equipment. The purchase was already made; the testing is post-hoc rather than time-critical. The excuse pattern mirrors the first: acquisition already complete, inspection merely convenient.
20: gynaika egēma kai dia touto ou dunamai elthein — I married a wife and therefore I cannot come — the third excuse inverts the polite formula of the first two (the others apologized: erōtō se, echē me parēitēmenon = please have me excused); this one is a flat refusal without apology. Deut 20:7 and 24:5 exempted a newly married man from military service, but a private banquet is not war. The marriage excuse is the most brazen: no apology offered, no return-commitment made. The escalating rudeness of the three excuses narrates the guests' growing disrespect for the host.
21: tote orgistheis ho oikodespotēs eipen tō doulō autou — the master became angry and said to his servant — orgizō (be angry — the full-anger word; also Matt 18:34; 22:7) signals that the honor violation is complete. exelthe tacheōs eis tas plateias kai rhumas tēs poleōs — go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the city — plateia (broad street, main avenue) vs rhymē (narrow alley, lane — the back streets); the servant is to scour both the public main streets and the private back alleys. The replacement guests are the same four categories as v.13: poor, crippled, blind, lame — the socially excluded become the feast's recipients.
22: gegonen ho epetaxas kai eti topos estin — what you ordered has been done and there is still room — topos (place, room, space) — the banquet hall has more capacity than the replacement guests have filled; the servant reports both compliance and remaining availability. The ongoing vacancy is the narrative setup for the third dispatch (v.23), expanding the invitation beyond the city to the roads and countryside. The feast's generosity exceeds even the first replacement population.
23: anankason eiselthein — compel them to come in — anankason (aorist active imperative of anankazō = compel, force, constrain — from anankē = necessity) is the disputed word. In the patristic period Augustine cited this verse for compelling heretics into the church; Calvin and others rejected this use. The Greek sense is "urge strongly, press insistently" — the cultural background is the Middle Eastern hospitality dynamic where hosts must overcome their guests' polite refusals with persistent invitation. Hina gemisthē mou ho oikos — so that my house will be full — the goal is fullness, abundance, the eschatological feast completed.
24: oudeis tōn andrōn ekeinōn tōn keklēmenōn geusetal mou tou deipnou — not one of those men who were invited will taste my banquet — geusasthai (taste, experience — from geuomai) with the genitive tou deipnou: taste of my banquet (partitive genitive). The guests who were initially invited and refused are now permanently excluded from what they declined. The Lukan context makes the parable's referent clear: the Jewish leadership who have been invited to the kingdom feast and refused will not participate, while those they considered socially unworthy fill the hall.
25: suneporeuonto de autō ochloi polloi — large crowds were travelling with him — the transitional verse marks a shift of audience from the Pharisee's dinner table to the road crowds. Suneporeuomai (travel together with — compound of sun- + poreuomai) implies the crowds are joining the journey movement; this is the Travel Narrative context (9:51ff). Strapheis eipen pros autous — turning he said to them — Luke's characteristic physical gesture-before-teaching (strephō = turn, also 7:9; 9:55; 10:23); the turn signals a direct and personally addressed teaching.
26: ei tis erchetai pros me kai ou misei ton patera heautou kai tēn mētera — if anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother — misei (hates — present indicative of miseō) is the strong word; its Semitic idiom background (comparative love: "hate" = "love less") is confirmed by the Matthew parallel (10:37: ho philōn patera ē mētera hyper eme = "who loves father or mother more than me"). The MKT "hate" renders the Aramaic underlying idiom faithfully while the Matthean version interprets it. Eti te kai tēn psychēn heautou — yes, even his own life — psychē (life, self) is added to the family list; self-attachment is the final attachment to be relativized by attachment to Jesus.
27: hostis ou bastazei ton stauron heautou — whoever does not carry his own cross — bastazō (carry, bear — the carrying verb for burdens; cf. Gal 6:5; John 19:17 for Jesus carrying his cross) rather than Luke 9:23's airō (take up); both verbs describe cross-bearing but with different emphases: airō = taking up, bastazō = sustained carrying. The cross-bearing here is the discipleship condition stated before the two parables of cost-counting (tower, king); it frames the calculations as the concrete form of cross-bearing.
28: tis ex hymōn thelōn purgon oikodomēsai — who among you, wanting to build a tower — purgos (tower — a watchtower, defensive tower, or tall farm-structure for guarding fields) as the construction project; the point is not architectural grandeur but any project requiring advance resource assessment. Kathisas prōton psēphizei tēn dapanēn — first sitting down counts the cost — psēphizō (count, calculate — from psēphos = pebble, voting-stone, used as a counting-token) is the financial calculation verb; dapanē (cost, expense — from dapanaō = spend, consume) is the sum to be counted before beginning.
29: hina mēpote thentos autou themelion kai mē ischuontos ektelesai — lest after laying the foundation and not being able to finish — themelion (foundation — from themis = law, established order; the base on which something stands) + ektelesai (finish completely — intensive compound of ek- + teleō = complete thoroughly). The incomplete tower is the parable's embarrassing failure case; the Lukan context suggests that half-committed discipleship — starting without counting the cost — produces the same visible failure.
30: houtos ho anthrōpos ērxato oikodomein kai ouk ischusen ektelesai — this man began to build and was not able to finish — the imperfect ērxato (began — past inception) + aorist ischusen (was able) creates the completed failure: he started (imperfect progress) but could not finish (aorist finality). Empaizein autō — mock him — empaizō (mock, ridicule — from en + paizō = play; playing at someone's expense) is the honor-destruction verb; the same word is used for the soldiers mocking Jesus (22:63; 23:11, 36).
31: ē tis basileus poreuomenos hetō basilei symbalein eis polemon — or what king, going to engage another king in war — the second parable of calculation shifts from private construction to royal military strategy. Symballō (engage, meet — literally "throw together"; the military idiom is "engage in battle") with eis polemon (into war). Ei dynatai en deka chiliasin hypantēsai tō meta eikosi chiliadeōn — whether he is able with ten thousand to meet the one coming with twenty thousand — the force ratio (1:2) makes the calculation concrete; the weaker king must assess before the armies meet.
32: ei de mēge, eti autou porrō ontos presbeian aposteilas erōta ta pros eirēnēn — if not, while the other is still far away, sending an embassy he asks for peace terms — presbeia (embassy, delegation — from presbys = old, elder; an embassy of elders) + aposteilas (having sent) + erōtaō ta pros eirēnēn (ask for the things toward peace). The king who cannot win asks for terms before the battle; the discipleship application is that following Jesus requires calculating whether one can sustain what is required — and if not, to surrender fully rather than partially.
33: pas ex hymōn hos ouk apotassetai pasin tois heautou hyparchousin — everyone who does not renounce all his own possessions — apotassomai (renounce, take leave of, bid farewell to — from apo- + tassō = put in order; to arrange for departure, hence renounce/relinquish) appears also in 9:61 (the third would-be disciple asking to say goodbye). Tois heautou hyparchousin (all his own belongings — hyparchō = exist, belong to) is the possessions-word used throughout Luke (8:3; 12:15, 33, 44; 16:1; 19:8). The application lands harder than the parables: the tower-builder counts the cost before building; the disciple must count the cost and then relinquish everything.
34: kalon oun to halas, ean de kai to halas mōranthē — salt is good, but if the salt becomes foolish/tasteless — halas (salt — the Greek word; also hals in some forms) is the agricultural and preservation material; in Galilee it was often impure, mixed with other minerals, and could lose its sodium chloride content through leaching, leaving only the tasteless mineral residue. Mōrainō (become foolish, become tasteless — from mōros = foolish, dull; the same root as English "moron") is the double-meaning word: salt that loses its saltiness has become "foolish" — the metaphor and the moral judgment are encoded in the same word.
35: oute eis gēn oute eis koprian eutheton estin — it is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile — euthetos (fit, suitable, useful — from eu- + tithēmi = well-placed) with the double negative: tasteless salt fails at both its primary use (seasoning, preservation) and as a soil amendment (salt was sometimes used in compost in small quantities, but insipid mineral residue has no value). ho echōn ōta akouein akouetō — whoever has ears to hear, let them hear — Luke's standard call-to-attention formula (also 8:8; 13:9); the salt metaphor concludes the discipleship section of chapter 14 with the same urgency-marker as the parable section of chapter 8.
